answer:
**Iqbal — strongest khudi verse in corpus:**

The corpus contains only Bang-e-Dara (Parts 1–3); Asrar-e-Khudi, Iqbal's primary khudi text, is not present. The closest verse in Bang-e-Dara to the claim that the self is built, not given, is in **Bang-e-Dara Part 3, Chapter 12 "Insaan" (The Human Being)** [allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8, ch. 12]:

> [p-cb3c43] "Whatever exists in the world is accustomed to submission; Every power of the Human Being is aflame with striving."
> [p-587d8c] "If it so chooses, it can change the very form of the garden; This being is wise, is perceptive, is potent."

This poem makes the key khudi distinction explicit: all other creation is fixed in submission (its nature is given), while the Human Being's powers are "aflame with striving" — i.e., constituted through action, not inheritance. The phrase "if it so chooses" asserts radical self-determination. This is as close as Bang-e-Dara comes to Asrar-e-Khudi's programmatic claim that khudi is forged through effort, love, and sustained will — not received by birth.

---

**Manu — strongest varna-as-given verse:**

**Manusmrti Chapter 1, verse Mn_1.31** [unknown-manusmrti-347b76, ch. 1, p-13a2db]:

> "And for the sake of the prosperity of the worlds, he caused the Brahmana, the Kshatriya, the Vaishya, and the Shudra to emanate from his mouth, arms, thighs, and feet."

This is the cosmogonic origin of the varna system. The four social classes literally proceed from the body of the Creator — mouth, arms, thighs, feet — making social location a cosmic fact of origin, not an achievement. The doctrine is reinforced immediately downstream at Mn_1.93 [p-7c0113]:

> "By his origin from the highest limb, by his seniority, and by his preservation of the Veda, the Brahmana is by right the lord of this whole creation."

Here, the ground of lordship is not merit but "origin from the highest limb." The self's social location is guaranteed by where in the Creator's body your class type originated — the most fully given claim possible.

---

**Which is harder to hold honestly?**

Manu's varna-dharma is the harder doctrine to hold honestly. Here is why:

Khudi's claim — that the self is constructed through striving — is at least empirically grounded in part. People do build themselves through disciplined effort, love, and sustained practice. The claim is aspirational and sets an impossibly high bar (most people don't fully build themselves), but it does not require believing something demonstrably false. The psychological difficulty of khudi is existential: it demands you own your self completely, including its mediocrity and failures. But as a belief, its epistemic footing is sound.

Varna-dharma's claim — that birth from a specific cosmic body-part determines your entire social location, worth, and duty — requires surrendering ordinary epistemic standards. To hold it honestly, a Brahmin must look at the actual distribution of capability, virtue, and insight across birth-castes and still say: "My pre-eminence is not earned, it is given by my origin from Brahma's mouth." This requires either deep pre-critical cosmological faith (rare and demanding) or motivated rationalization of hereditary privilege as cosmic justice.

The Manusmrti itself quietly undermines the absolute doctrine. At Mn_10.65 [p-ced8db], Manu concedes: "A Shudra attains the state of a Brahmana, and a Brahmana attains the state of a Shudra" — by which he means across seven generations of mixed birth, but still: the text admits hereditary lines are not perfectly rigid. Mn_1.31's clean cosmogonic certainty is already in tension with Mn_10's more complex genealogical accounting.

In sum: khudi is the harder doctrine to *practice* (it demands relentless self-construction, no blame-shifting). Varna-dharma is the harder doctrine to *hold honestly* — it asserts that cosmic genetics determines human worth, a claim that honest confrontation with evidence, and with one's own moral intuition, cannot sustain without bad faith.

tool_calls.result_summary: Found 3 works: Bang-e-Dara Parts 1, 2, 3 (all Urdu). No Asrar-e-Khudi or Persian works in corpus.

tool_calls.name: search_corpus
tool_calls.result_summary: 17 hits, all Ghalib/Zauq transliterations using 'be-khudi' (self-loss, not self-construction). No Iqbal khudi results.

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Insaan (Human Being): contrasts all creation's 'submission' with human 'striving'; 'if it so chooses, it can change the very form of the garden.' Closest khudi verse in corpus.

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Sarguzasht-e-Adam: Adam as restless builder and discoverer; human agency across history. Thematically khudi-adjacent but not the sharpest formulation.

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Koshish-e-Natamam: 'Through striving without end, all living things remain.' Related theme but about cosmic nature, not human self-construction specifically.

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Mn_1.31: four varnas emanate from Brahma's mouth/arms/thighs/feet. Mn_1.93: Brahmin's lordship comes from origin from highest limb. Mn_1.98-99: Brahmin birth = eternal incarnation of Dharma. Clearest birth-determines-social-location passage.

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Detailed varna-mixing rules. Mn_10.65 allows Shudra-to-Brahmin mobility across seven generations, which slightly complicates the Ch.1 absolute determinism. Mn_10.97: 'Better one's own dharma, even if imperfect, than another's well-performed.'
